2026-03-18T16:22:45.2485354Z     1375. 						// Check if the owner struct is an incomplete template instantiation
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2486182Z     1376. 						auto owner_type_it = gTypesByName.find(owner_struct->getName());
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2487178Z     1377. 						if (owner_type_it != gTypesByName.end() && owner_type_it->second->is_incomplete_instantiation_) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2488287Z     1378. 							std::string_view owner_name = StringTable::getStringView(owner_struct->getName());
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2489327Z     1379. 							FLASH_LOG(Codegen, Error, "Cannot access static member '", qualifiedIdNode.name(),
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2490243Z     1380. 							"' from incomplete template instantiation '", owner_name, "'");
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2491028Z     1381. 							// Return a placeholder value instead of generating GlobalLoad
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2491938Z     1382. 							// This prevents linker errors from undefined references to incomplete instantiations
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2492850Z     1383. 							return makeExprResult(Type::Bool, SizeInBits{8}, 0ULL);
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2493430Z     1384. 						}
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2493740Z     1385. 
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2494143Z     1386. 						// Determine the correct qualified name to use
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2494944Z     1387. 						// If we accessed through a type alias (struct_type_it->second) that resolves to
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2496061Z     1388. 						// a different struct than the owner, we should use the resolved struct name
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2496920Z     1389. 						StringHandle qualified_struct_name = owner_struct->getName();
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2497540Z     1390. 
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2498017Z     1391. 						// Check if we're accessing through a type alias by comparing names
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2498827Z     1392. 						if (struct_type_it->second->name() != owner_struct->getName()) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2499554Z     1393. 							// Accessing through type alias or derived class
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2500491Z     1394. 							// First, check if this is inheritance (owner_struct is a base class of accessed struct)
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2501804Z     1395. 							// In that case, we should use owner_struct's name directly, not do type alias resolution
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2502600Z     1396. 							bool is_inheritance = false;
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2503343Z     1397. 							const StructTypeInfo* accessed_struct = struct_type_it->second->getStructInfo();
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2504093Z     1398. 							if (accessed_struct) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2504649Z     1399. 								for (const auto& base : accessed_struct->base_classes) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2505484Z     1400. 									if (base.type_index.value < gTypeInfo.size()) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2506177Z     1401. 										const TypeInfo& base_type = gTypeInfo[base.type_index.value];
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2506933Z     1402. 										const StructTypeInfo* base_struct = base_type.getStructInfo();
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2507721Z     1403. 										if (base_struct && base_struct->getName() == owner_struct->getName()) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2508413Z     1404. 											is_inheritance = true;
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2509415Z     1405. 											FLASH_LOG(Codegen, Debug, "Static member found via inheritance from base class: ", owner_struct->getName());
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2510313Z     1406. 											break;
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2510669Z     1407. 										}
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2510964Z     1408. 									}
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2511262Z     1409. 								}
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2511602Z     1410. 							}
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2511893Z     1411. 
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2512386Z     1412. 							// Skip type alias resolution for inheritance - use owner_struct's name directly
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2513086Z     1413. 							if (!is_inheritance) {
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2513581Z     1414. 								// Try to resolve to the actual instantiated type
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2514196Z     1415. 								const TypeInfo* resolved_type = struct_type_it->second;
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2514730Z     1416. 
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2535331Z     1417. 								// Special handling for true_type and false_type
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2536221Z     1418. 								// These should resolve to integral_constant<bool, 1> and integral_constant<bool, 0>
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2537126Z     1419. 								// but the template system doesn't instantiate them properly
2026-03-18T16:22:45.2538034Z     1420. 								std::string_view alias_name = StringTable::getStringView(resolved_type->name());
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6287062Z 
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6287834Z copilot: Looking at this code, the issue with typedef enum aliases is different from what I thought:
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6288551Z 
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6288797Z When `Container::AliasStatus::Ok` is accessed:
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6289442Z 1. `struct_or_enum_name` = "AliasStatus" (from namespace registry last component)
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6291163Z 2. At line 1272: `gTypesByName.find("AliasStatus")` → might NOT be found (only "Container::AliasStatus" is registered, with the Container:: prefix)
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6308361Z 3. So it falls through to check depth, finds "Container::AliasStatus" with depth > 1 check
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6309358Z 4. `struct_type_it` = TypeInfo for "Container::AliasStatus" which is Type::Enum
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6309906Z 
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6309912Z 
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6310837Z 5. The code finds the full qualified name at line 1300, but then the struct check at line 1360 fails because it's an enum, not a struct, so the logic doesn't handle this case properly.
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6311900Z 
2026-03-18T16:23:32.6314671Z The real problem is that the enum lookup only searches for the simple name "AliasStatus" in gTypesByName, which doesn't exist—only the fully qualified "Container::AliasStatus" is registered. I need to either register the simple alias name alongside the qualified one, or after finding the qualified TypeInfo, check if it's an enum and handle the value lookup accordingly.

2026-03-18T16:23:38.4468846Z     3476. 	ScopedTokenPosition saved_position(*this);
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4469633Z     3477. 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4470709Z     3478. 	// Consume 'enum' keyword
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4471206Z     3479. 	auto enum_keyword = advance();
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4471619Z     3480. 	if (enum_keyword.kind() != "enum"_tok) {
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4472438Z     3481. 		return ParseResult::error("Expected 'enum' keyword", enum_keyword);
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4473034Z     3482. 	}
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4473234Z     3483. 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4473667Z     3484. 	// Check for 'class' or 'struct' keyword (enum class / enum struct)
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4474122Z     3485. 	bool is_scoped = false;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4474486Z     3486. 	if (peek().is_keyword() &&
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4474848Z     3487. 	    (peek() == "class"_tok || peek() == "struct"_tok)) {
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4475552Z     3488. 		is_scoped = true;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4475869Z     3489. 		advance(); // consume 'class' or 'struct'
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4476217Z     3490. 	}
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4476514Z     3491. 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4476761Z     3492. 	// Parse enum name (optional for anonymous enums)
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4477266Z     3493. 	StringHandle enum_name;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4477693Z     3494. 	//bool is_anonymous = false;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4478096Z     3495. 	
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4478590Z     3496. 	// Check if next token is an identifier (name) or : or { (anonymous enum)
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4479258Z     3497. 	if (peek().is_identifier()) {
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4479711Z     3498. 		auto name_token = advance();
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4480157Z     3499. 		enum_name = name_token.handle();
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4480628Z     3500. 	} else if (!peek().is_eof() && 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4481120Z     3501. 	           (peek() == ":"_tok || peek() == "{"_tok)) {
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4481672Z     3502. 		// Anonymous enum - generate a unique name
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4482191Z     3503. 		static int anonymous_enum_counter = 0;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4483344Z     3504. 		enum_name = StringTable::getOrInternStringHandle(StringBuilder().append("__anonymous_enum_").append(std::to_string(anonymous_enum_counter++)));
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4484480Z     3505. 		//is_anonymous = true;
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4485003Z     3506. 	} else {
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4485534Z     3507. 		return ParseResult::error("Expected enum name, ':', or '{'", peek_info());
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4485947Z     3508. 	}
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4486124Z     3509. 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4486384Z     3510. 	// Register the enum type in the global type system EARLY
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4486917Z     3511. 	NamespaceHandle enum_namespace_handle = gSymbolTable.get_current_namespace_handle();
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4487509Z     3512. 	TypeInfo& enum_type_info = add_enum_type(enum_name, enum_namespace_handle);
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4487898Z     3513. 
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4488088Z     3514. 	// Create enum declaration node
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4488533Z     3515. 	auto [enum_node, enum_ref] = emplace_node_ref<EnumDeclarationNode>(enum_name, is_scoped);
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4489098Z     3516. 	// Bind this AST node directly to its TypeInfo so codegen never needs to
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4489612Z     3517. 	// search by name (which would collide when two functions define local enums
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4490710Z     3518. 	// with the same unqualified name — gTypesByName::emplace is a no-op on duplicates).
2026-03-18T16:23:38.4491252Z     3519. 	enum_ref.set_type_index(enum_type_info.type_index_);

2026-03-18T16:24:54.3249219Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3250577Z copilot: So the enum is registered with `add_enum_type(enum_name, ...)` where `enum_name` is the SIMPLE name (from the token). So the enum "Status" inside struct "Container" in namespace "ns" is registered as:
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3252379Z - `gTypesByName["Status"]` → TypeInfo for Status
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3252766Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3253207Z That's the simple name. Then in our new code after enum parsing (lines 751-788), we also register:
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3254110Z - `gTypesByName["Container::Status"]` (struct-relative)
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3254782Z - `gTypesByName["ns::Container::Status"]` (namespace-qualified)
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3255493Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3256223Z For the typedef alias `using AliasStatus = Status`, `register_type_alias("Container::AliasStatus", type_spec_for_Status)` registers:
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3257113Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3257120Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3257573Z - `gTypesByName["Container::AliasStatus"]` → TypeInfo for alias
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3258037Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3260512Z The issue is that when the codegen looks for "AliasStatus" as a simple name in the enum check at line 1272, it fails because only the qualified "Container::AliasStatus" is registered. But directly declared enums like "Status" work because they're registered under their simple name too. I need to trace through how `check_status(ns::Container::Status::Ok)` actually resolves to understand why the simple name lookup succeeds for enums but not aliases.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3263284Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3265513Z Looking at the namespace resolution, when we extract just "Status" from the qualified name and search `gTypesByName`, we find it because `add_enum_type` registers unscoped enums by simple name. The enum check passes, but since it's unscoped, the `is_scoped` condition at line 1278 is false and execution falls through to the struct validation at line 1287.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3267629Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3269517Z Then when we try the fully qualified lookup "Container::Status" at line 1317, we do find it in the registry, but the struct type check at line 1360 fails because it's an enum, not a struct. So we're stuck—the code doesn't have a path forward for unscoped enums in this scenario.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3271171Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3273203Z Looking at the actual error output, the real problem surfaces when we call `check_alias` with the enumerator `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`—the codegen can't resolve "Ok" as a variable in scope, which means the enumerator lookup is failing during the function call argument resolution.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3275263Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3277431Z The root cause is that `AliasStatus` is just a type alias pointing to the original `Status` type, but when we try to access `AliasStatus::Ok`, the codegen isn't properly following the alias to find the enumerators that actually belong to `Status`. The enumerator information exists in the original type, not in the alias itself, so the lookup chain is breaking somewhere. EnumTypeInfo
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3280166Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3281441Z But the lookup at line 1272 finds "AliasStatus" in gTypesByName, which resolves to "Container::AliasStatus" through the depth check. The problem is this returns a Type::Enum TypeInfo that represents the alias itself, not the original type.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3282875Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3284368Z When `register_type_alias` is called, the alias TypeInfo gets the type_index of the original "Status" type. But when the code tries to retrieve enumerators at line 1276, it calls `getEnumInfo()` on the alias TypeInfo, which returns null since aliases don't copy the EnumTypeInfo data.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3286232Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3287798Z So even though we find "Container::AliasStatus" as a struct_type at line 1297, it's actually an enum, which causes the check at line 1360 to fail. I need to trace what happens after that point when we have an enum but the struct validation fails.
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3315887Z 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3316348Z function:

2026-03-18T16:24:54.3362990Z     1567. 					if (!is_array_type) {

2026-03-18T16:24:54.3364961Z     1570. 							type_node.type(), size_bits));
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3395803Z     1571. 					}
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3396288Z     1572. 
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3396737Z     1573. 				// Return the temp variable that will hold the loaded value
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3397694Z     1574. 				TypeIndex type_index = (type_node.type() == Type::Struct) ? type_node.type_index() : TypeIndex{};
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3398909Z     1575. 					return makeExprResult(type_node.type(), SizeInBits{size_bits}, IrOperand{result_temp}, type_index);
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3399752Z     1576. 			} else {
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3400398Z     1577. 				// Local variable - just return the name
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3401254Z     1578. 				TypeIndex type_index = (type_node.type() == Type::Struct) ? type_node.type_index() : TypeIndex{};
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3402981Z     1579. 				return makeExprResult(type_node.type(), SizeInBits{static_cast<int>(type_node.size_in_bits())}, IrOperand{StringTable::getOrInternStringHandle(qualifiedIdNode.name())}, type_index);
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3404322Z     1580. 			}
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3404611Z     1581. 		}
2026-03-18T16:24:54.3404882Z     1582.

2026-03-18T16:28:22.9028212Z copilot: Now I have a complete understanding of all three issues. Let me implement the fixes:
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9028798Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9029125Z **Fix 1**: Nested struct type_name building (lines 100-116) - walk full context stack + prepend namespace
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9029946Z **Fix 2**: typedef/using alias registration - register simple name + ns-qualified name  
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9030729Z **Fix 3**: No-mangled-name dedup - use function source name as fallback identity
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9031098Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9031249Z Let me start:
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9034485Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9034881Z copilot: Looking at the code flow for `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`:
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9035678Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9036890Z 1. Lines 1271-1285: Check for simple name "AliasStatus" as scoped enum → not found (only "Container::AliasStatus" registered)
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9037867Z 2. Lines 1290-1358: Look for struct type...
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9038539Z    - depth > 1 check: finds "Container::AliasStatus" via namespace registry
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9039418Z    - `struct_type_it` = TypeInfo for "Container::AliasStatus" (Type::Enum)
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9040275Z 3. Line 1360: `struct_type_it->second->isStruct()` = false, falls through
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9040804Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9040810Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9041960Z 4. Lines 1524-1538: Attempts to look up "AliasStatus::Ok" in the symbol table but doesn't find it, then returns a placeholder with "Ok" as a variable name, which triggers the "Missing variable: Ok" error.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9043238Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9044799Z The core issue is that when the alias resolves to an enum type, the code path doesn't handle extracting enum values—it only checks if the resolved type is a struct, so enum aliases fall through without proper handling.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9046645Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9049973Z Now tracing through the direct `Status::Ok` case, I'm looking at how the namespace registry resolves the enum itself versus the qualified identifier components. The key difference seems to be in how the depth check interprets the namespace for `Container::Status` when it's part of the enum's own namespace versus when it's accessed through an alias. Now I'm checking whether the namespace depth validation actually works—if "Container::Status" has depth 2, the check passes, and then I'm verifying whether that qualified name exists in the types registry. Looking at the test results, the original hidden friend enum test passed after our fix, which suggests the enum is being registered correctly, but I need to trace through whether the typedef case is also working or if there's still an issue there.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9053102Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9054839Z The key difference I'm seeing is that simple names like "Status" get registered in the types map, so they're found by the scoped enum check, but "AliasStatus" isn't registered as a simple name—only the qualified "Container::AliasStatus" is. So I need to modify the type alias registration to also store the simple name alongside the qualified name.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9056569Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9058498Z Modifying the registration logic... For type aliases inside structs, I need to register not just the qualified name but also the simple name to match how enums work. The fix is to capture the returned TypeInfo reference from `register_type_alias` and use it to add additional registrations for both the simple name and the namespace-qualified variant.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9060191Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9060309Z Building the registration logic...
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9060628Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9062520Z Actually, I'm realizing there are multiple call sites for `register_type_alias` scattered throughout `parse_member_type_alias` — at least six different locations — and I need to check whether all of them are actually within struct contexts or if some are being called outside of structs where this registration logic wouldn't apply.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9063943Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9065376Z Looking at each call site more carefully, some have struct_ref checks while others might not, so the cleanest approach would be to add the simple name and namespace-qualified name registration right after each `register_type_alias` call whenever `struct_ref` is provided, rather than trying to handle this in one centralized place.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9066577Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9067482Z The real question is whether we need to distinguish between enum and struct aliases, since the TypeInfo will have the correct type_ field. For ADL and enumerator lookup to work properly, I need to make sure the simple name gets registered in the struct_ref branch specifically.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9068724Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9069840Z Let me focus on what Devin's comment is actually asking for: verifying that typedef'd nested enums work correctly with ADL, which means I need to register both the simple name for codegen to find enumerators and the namespace-qualified name for ADL resolution.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9070905Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9074762Z Looking at the code flow, the qualified-name registration only happens when the enum keyword appears directly in the struct member parsing loop, but typedef and using aliases that reference those enums bypass that registration entirely. So for a case like `struct Container { enum Status { Ok, Fail }; typedef Status StatusAlias; }`, we register Container::Status and ns::Container::Status for the direct enum, but we don't register Container::StatusAlias or ns::Container::StatusAlias for the alias. The type_alias registration handles the basic lookup, but we're missing the qualified-name variants for the alias itself.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9079161Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9081231Z The real problem isn't ADL—it's that when the codegen tries to look up enumerators through the alias, it can't find the right qualified context. I need to focus on fixing the nested struct type_name handling first, since that's critical for both 2-level and 3-level nested struct access.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9083039Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9084894Z For the typedef alias issue, I should register the simple name "AliasStatus" alongside the qualified name so codegen can find enumerators. There's also the namespace-qualified alias case to handle for full ADL support, and a latent edge case with deduplication that could use the function name as a fallback identity.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9087124Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9088883Z The concrete changes needed are: modifying lines 100-116 for nested struct type_name, updating the registration logic in `parse_member_type_alias` to include the simple name, and handling the namespace-qualified case. Let me figure out the cleanest approach for Fix 2 across the multiple call sites.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9091047Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9097401Z Actually, the right strategy is to modify `parse_member_type_alias` so when we build the qualified name, we also register both the simple alias name and the namespace-qualified version—mirroring how `add_enum_type` handles direct enum declarations. But I need to be careful with Fix 1 since changing the nested struct type_name could have broad impact. Let me trace through what happens currently when B is nested inside ns::A. work correctly for nested classes without a namespace—the qualified name "A::B" gets registered and lookups find it through the full stack walk. ✓ I'm confident this fix handles both namespaced and non-namespaced nested structs, and the namespace prefix search in `lookupTypeInCurrentContext` already provides backward compatibility without needing explicit aliases.
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9102481Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9102814Z Now I'll implement the fixes, starting with the highest-impact changes.

2026-03-18T16:28:22.9111273Z     89. 	
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9111728Z     90. 	// Get namespace handle and qualified name early so we can use it for both TypeInfo and StructTypeInfo
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9112410Z     91. 	NamespaceHandle current_namespace_handle = gSymbolTable.get_current_namespace_handle();
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9113121Z     92. 	std::string_view qualified_namespace = gNamespaceRegistry.getQualifiedName(current_namespace_handle);
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9113624Z     93. 	
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9113856Z     94. 	// Build the full qualified name for use in mangling
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9114200Z     95. 	// - For nested classes: Parent::Child
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9114510Z     96. 	// - For namespace classes: ns::Class  
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9114894Z     97. 	// - For top-level classes: just the simple name
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9115498Z     98. 	StringHandle full_qualified_name;
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9115752Z     99. 	
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9115946Z     100. 	if (is_nested_class) {
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9116242Z     101. 		// We're inside a struct, so this is a nested class
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9116668Z     102. 		// Use the qualified name (e.g., "Outer::Inner") for the TypeInfo entry
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9117131Z     103. 		const auto& context = struct_parsing_context_stack_.back();
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9117607Z     104. 		// Build the qualified name using StringBuilder for a persistent allocation
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9118156Z     105. 		qualified_struct_name = StringTable::getOrInternStringHandle(StringBuilder()
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9118584Z     106. 			.append(context.struct_name)
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9118848Z     107. 			.append("::")
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9119074Z     108. 			.append(struct_name));
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9119339Z     109. 		type_name = qualified_struct_name;
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9119652Z     110. 		full_qualified_name = qualified_struct_name;
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9119990Z     111. 	} else if (!qualified_namespace.empty()) {
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9120653Z     112. 		// Top-level class in a namespace - use namespace-qualified name for proper mangling
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9121369Z     113. 		full_qualified_name = gNamespaceRegistry.buildQualifiedIdentifier(current_namespace_handle, struct_name);
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9122165Z     114. 		qualified_struct_name = full_qualified_name;  // Also update qualified_struct_name for implicit constructors
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9122833Z     115. 		type_name = full_qualified_name;  // TypeInfo should also use fully qualified name
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9123237Z     116. 	}
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9123408Z     117. 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9123740Z     118. 	TypeInfo& struct_type_info = add_struct_type(type_name, current_namespace_handle);
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9124160Z     119. 
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9124502Z     120. 	// For nested classes, also register with the simple name so it can be referenced
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9125009Z     121. 	// from within the nested class itself (e.g., in constructors)
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9125678Z     122. 	if (is_nested_class) {
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9126003Z     123. 		gTypesByName.emplace(struct_name, &struct_type_info);
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9126323Z     124. 	}
2026-03-18T16:28:22.9126488Z     125.
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6611977Z 
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6626268Z function:
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6627041Z   name: edit
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6627675Z   args:
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6628936Z     new_str: |
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6629532Z       	if (is_nested_class) {
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6630453Z       		// We're inside a struct, so this is a nested class.
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6631221Z       		// Build the full struct chain by walking the context stack from outermost to
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6632175Z       		// innermost parent.  Each context entry stores the simple (source-level) struct
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6633118Z       		// name, so concatenating them gives the correct chain at every depth, e.g.:
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6633904Z       		//   depth-1:  "A::B"         (context=[A], current="B")
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6634568Z       		//   depth-2:  "A::B::C"      (context=[A,B], current="C")
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6635554Z       		// The previous code only looked at back() so it produced "B::C" for depth-2.
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6636280Z       		StringBuilder struct_chain_builder;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6636857Z       		for (const auto& ctx : struct_parsing_context_stack_) {
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6637560Z       			struct_chain_builder.append(ctx.struct_name).append("::");
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6638142Z       		}
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6638501Z       		struct_chain_builder.append(struct_name);
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6639411Z       		StringHandle struct_chain = StringTable::getOrInternStringHandle(struct_chain_builder.commit());
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6640263Z       
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6640599Z       		if (!qualified_namespace.empty()) {
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6641128Z       			// Namespace + struct chain: "ns::A::B::C"
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6641850Z       			full_qualified_name = gNamespaceRegistry.buildQualifiedIdentifier(
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6642612Z       				current_namespace_handle, struct_chain);
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6643196Z       			qualified_struct_name = full_qualified_name;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6643762Z       			type_name = full_qualified_name;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6644208Z       		} else {
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6644669Z       			// No enclosing namespace: just the struct chain "A::B::C"
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6645478Z       			qualified_struct_name = struct_chain;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6645972Z       			type_name = struct_chain;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6646423Z       			full_qualified_name = struct_chain;
2026-03-18T16:28:47.6646854Z       		}

2026-03-18T16:30:17.7270678Z copilot: Now fix Issue 3 - the no-mangled-name dedup. Let me look at the existing code and add a fallback using the function source name + declaration address:
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7306913Z 
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7307563Z function:
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7308130Z   name: view
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7309444Z   args:
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7311201Z     path: /home/runner/work/FlashCpp/FlashCpp/src/OverloadResolution.h
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7312110Z     view_range: |
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7312766Z       [
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7313293Z         1470,
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7313976Z         1500
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7314536Z       ]
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7315229Z   result: |
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7315787Z     1470. 
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7317675Z     1471. 		// Build a set of existing mangled names for O(1) dedup.
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7318475Z     1472. 		// Using std::string_view is safe: mangled names are interned in
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7319291Z     1473. 		// stable ChunkedStringAllocator storage (they never move).
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7320086Z     1474. 		std::unordered_set<std::string_view> existing_mangled;
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7320757Z     1475. 		existing_mangled.reserve(overloads.size());
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7321341Z     1476. 		for (const auto& node : overloads) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7321918Z     1477. 			if (node.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7322579Z     1478. 				const auto& fd = node.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7323195Z     1479. 				if (fd.has_mangled_name()) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7323749Z     1480. 					existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name());
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7324278Z     1481. 				}
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7324586Z     1482. 			}
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7332669Z     1483. 		}
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7333087Z     1484. 		for (auto& cand : adl_candidates) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7333663Z     1485. 			if (cand.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7334321Z     1486. 				const auto& fd = cand.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7335287Z     1487. 				// If the function has no mangled name we cannot deduplicate it
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7336427Z     1488. 				// reliably; include it so we don't silently drop valid candidates.
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7337282Z     1489. 				if (!fd.has_mangled_name() || existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name()).second) {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7337773Z     1490. 					overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7338067Z     1491. 				}
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7338270Z     1492. 			} else {
2026-03-18T16:30:17.7338524Z     1493. 				overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));

2026-03-18T16:31:21.6096342Z 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6098010Z copilot: So `DeclarationNode::nameHandle()` returns the function name as a StringHandle. Since StringHandle is an interned string, it's stable. For functions without a mangled name, we can use `fd.decl_node().nameHandle()` as a fallback identity key.
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6099022Z 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6100253Z But wait - the issue with pointer-based dedup was that `lookup_all()` and `lookup_adl()` return COPIES of ASTNodes, not references. So the same logical function could return two different FunctionDeclarationNode objects with different addresses but same content.
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6101245Z 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6101481Z For the mangled-name dedup, mangled names are interned strings in stable storage. 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6101811Z 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6103150Z For functions without mangled names, I could use the name handle as a fallback, though that's not perfect since different functions can share the same name. Adding parameter count would help distinguish overloads, but for most cases like operator overloads, the name handle alone might be sufficient since same name and parameter count typically means the same function or ambiguous candidates anyway.
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6104582Z 
2026-03-18T16:31:21.6106332Z Actually, I'm reconsidering using the function declaration node's address as a stable identifier—the original code compared pointers directly, but I need to verify whether the ASTNodes returned by lookup are copies of wrappers around stable underlying nodes or if they're truly ephemeral copies that would make pointer comparison unreliable.

2026-03-18T16:31:36.9390237Z     88. };
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2118000Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2119130Z copilot: ASTNode stores a `T*` pointer via `std::any`, so pointer comparisons are stable. Let me fix Issue 3 by using pointer-based fallback when no mangled name is available:
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2120452Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2122682Z copilot: Now I see! ASTNode stores a `std::any node_` which holds a `T*` pointer to the actual node (which lives in `gChunkedAnyStorage`). When you "copy" an ASTNode, you copy the `std::any` which contains the same pointer. So `cand.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>()` returns the SAME `FunctionDeclarationNode&` for the same underlying node, regardless of whether the ASTNode was copied.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2125529Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2127416Z This means `&cand.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>()` (pointer to the underlying object) IS stable and comparable! The original dedup was technically correct in terms of pointer comparison (same FunctionDeclarationNode object), but Gemini was wrong about it being a use-after-free bug.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2129402Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2129408Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2132583Z Gemini's claim that pointer comparison doesn't work because `lookup_all` and `lookup_adl` return copies is actually incorrect—copying an ASTNode just copies the pointer inside the `std::any`, not the object itself, so the addresses remain stable. The second concern about vector reallocation invalidating pointers in the set is the real issue to investigate.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2134332Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2137251Z Actually, wait—we're not storing pointers to `overloads` vector elements, we're storing pointers to the underlying FunctionDeclarationNodes in `gChunkedAnyStorage`, so reallocation of `overloads` doesn't affect them at all. The original pointer-based dedup was sound, though we've already switched to mangled-name-based dedup which is also correct but creates an edge case for functions without mangled names. I should add a fallback that uses pointer comparison when a mangled name isn't available, since we now know the ASTNode pointers are stable.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2140186Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2142084Z Writing fallback dedup logic... The current logic already covers the main cases—functions without mangled names pass through while those with mangled names get deduplicated by name. Since Devin mentioned that most operator overloads will have mangled names in practice, I could add pointer-based deduplication as a fallback for the edge case where mangled names aren't available.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2241083Z 
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2241917Z function:
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2242381Z   name: edit
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2242761Z   args:
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2244230Z     new_str: |
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2244681Z       		// Build a set of existing mangled names for O(1) dedup.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2245596Z       		// Using std::string_view is safe: mangled names are interned in
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2246384Z       		// stable ChunkedStringAllocator storage (they never move).
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2250395Z       		// ASTNode stores a T* pointer via std::any, so &fd is stable across
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2251811Z       		// copies and vector reallocations of overloads — pointer dedup is safe
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2252624Z       		// as a fallback for functions that do not yet have a mangled name.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2253381Z       		std::unordered_set<std::string_view> existing_mangled;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2254143Z       		std::unordered_set<const FunctionDeclarationNode*> existing_ptrs;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2254806Z       		existing_mangled.reserve(overloads.size());
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2255556Z       		existing_ptrs.reserve(overloads.size());
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2256092Z       		for (const auto& node : overloads) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2256624Z       			if (node.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2257216Z       				const auto& fd = node.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2257770Z       				if (fd.has_mangled_name()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2258252Z       					existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name());
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2258734Z       				} else {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2259284Z       					existing_ptrs.insert(&fd);
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2259700Z       				}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2259986Z       			}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2260266Z       		}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2260585Z       		for (auto& cand : adl_candidates) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2261097Z       			if (cand.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2261770Z       				const auto& fd = cand.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2262316Z       				bool is_duplicate;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2262722Z       				if (fd.has_mangled_name()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2263307Z       					// Dedup by mangled name (primary, semantically correct identity)
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2264086Z       					is_duplicate = !existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name()).second;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2264688Z       				} else {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2265549Z       					// Fallback: dedup by pointer identity (stable — ASTNode stores T*)
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2266287Z       					is_duplicate = !existing_ptrs.insert(&fd).second;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2266809Z       				}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2267114Z       				if (!is_duplicate) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2267564Z       					overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2268009Z       				}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2268292Z       			} else {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2268669Z       				overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2269430Z       			}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2269720Z       		}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2270002Z     old_str: |
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2270430Z       		// Build a set of existing mangled names for O(1) dedup.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2271111Z       		// Using std::string_view is safe: mangled names are interned in
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2271832Z       		// stable ChunkedStringAllocator storage (they never move).
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2272519Z       		std::unordered_set<std::string_view> existing_mangled;
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2273120Z       		existing_mangled.reserve(overloads.size());
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2273644Z       		for (const auto& node : overloads) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2274166Z       			if (node.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2274763Z       				const auto& fd = node.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2275508Z       				if (fd.has_mangled_name()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2276031Z       					existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name());
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2276765Z       				}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2277058Z       			}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2277332Z       		}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2277649Z       		for (auto& cand : adl_candidates) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2278159Z       			if (cand.is<FunctionDeclarationNode>()) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2278767Z       				const auto& fd = cand.as<FunctionDeclarationNode>();
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2279443Z       				// If the function has no mangled name we cannot deduplicate it
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2280218Z       				// reliably; include it so we don't silently drop valid candidates.
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2281075Z       				if (!fd.has_mangled_name() || existing_mangled.insert(fd.mangled_name()).second) {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2281818Z       					overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2282267Z       				}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2282556Z       			} else {
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2282917Z       				overloads.push_back(std::move(cand));
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2283378Z       			}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2283665Z       		}
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2284184Z     path: /home/runner/work/FlashCpp/FlashCpp/src/OverloadResolution.h
2026-03-18T16:32:35.2285360Z   result: File /home/runner/work/FlashCpp/FlashCpp/src/OverloadResolution.h updated with changes.

2026-03-18T16:35:57.4360565Z copilot: There's a crash! The changes I made broke something. Let me look at the crash log.
2026-03-18T16:35:57.8459602Z 
2026-03-18T16:35:57.8460154Z function:

2026-03-18T16:36:07.8432826Z copilot: The crash is an assertion failure in the standard library heap allocator. This is likely a use-after-free or corrupted memory due to our changes. Let me check what's different.
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8447346Z 
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8449442Z The problem might be in `parse_member_type_alias` - specifically in how I changed the code to register type aliases. The issue might be that after calling `register_type_alias(struct_relative_handle, ...)`, the returned `TypeInfo&` might be invalidated when we then call `gTypesByName.emplace(...)` which could potentially trigger a resize of `gTypeInfo` (if it's a deque or vector).
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8451577Z 
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8451879Z Actually, `gTypeInfo` is a `std::deque` (I think), which doesn't invalidate references. 
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8452383Z 
2026-03-18T16:36:07.8453162Z But if it's a `std::vector`, calling `emplace_back` could invalidate all references, so I need to verify what container type it actually is.

2026-03-18T16:36:26.6725611Z copilot: `gTypeInfo` is a `std::deque`, which does NOT invalidate references or iterators to existing elements when you insert at the back (`push_back` or `emplace_back`). So that's not the issue.
2026-03-18T16:36:26.6726756Z 
2026-03-18T16:36:26.6727601Z Wait, but what about `gTypesByName`? It's an `std::unordered_map`. When you call `emplace` on it, it doesn't invalidate existing iterators or references. So that's fine too.

2026-03-18T16:37:27.5145268Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5147515Z copilot: The crash happens when trying to access `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`. Let me look at what code path processes this type. The type `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok` - this would be parsed as a qualified identifier with namespace `ns::Container::AliasStatus` and name `Ok`. Let me see what happens in the type specifier when it encounters `ns::Container::AliasStatus` as a type and then tries to access `::Ok`.
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5149508Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5150396Z Actually wait, maybe the crash is in the type lookup after our enum registration. Let me check:When we parse `ns::Container::AliasStatus s`, the type specifier looks up "ns::Container::AliasStatus". Our code now registers:
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5151376Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5151380Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5151726Z - `struct_relative_handle` = "Container::AliasStatus" via `register_type_alias`
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5152231Z - `alias_name` = "AliasStatus" via `gTypesByName.emplace`
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5152834Z - `ns_qualified_handle` = "ns::Container::AliasStatus" via `gTypesByName.emplace`
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5153226Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5154249Z When we call `register_type_alias` with the struct-relative handle and final type spec, it creates a new TypeInfo that copies the type index from the final type spec.
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5155013Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5157540Z Now I'm tracing what `type_result.node()` actually is. For the `using AliasStatus = Status` declaration inside `struct Container`, the type specifier should resolve to the Status enum with its corresponding type index. The key question is whether the struct parsing context stack properly captures Container when we're building the qualified name chain—if Container is on the stack, we'd construct the full chain starting from there.
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5159435Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5160353Z When we register this type alias, we're creating a new TypeInfo entry with the qualified name "Container::AliasStatus", the enum type, and the Status type index. This should correctly represent the alias as a member of the Container struct.
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5161484Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5164883Z The SIGABRT crash is suspicious though—that usually points to heap corruption like a double-free or use-after-free. I'm wondering if the issue is in how `buildQualifiedIdentifier` is constructing the namespace-qualified handle, or if there's something going wrong with the map insertions that's corrupting memory. Actually, I think the issue might be something else. Let me think about what `parse_member_type_alias` receives in `final_type_spec`. Looking at the code, `final_type_spec` is a reference to a TypeSpecifierNode stored in `gChunkedAnyStorage`, which should be stable. But I'm wondering if there's a problem with registering it multiple times.
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5167943Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:27.5169459Z In `register_type_alias`, we're emplacing into `gTypesByName` twice—once with the struct-relative handle and once with the alias name—both pointing to the same info object. That should be fine, but let me trace through what's actually happening by running the test with strace to see where the crash occurs.
2026-03-18T16:37:28.4271943Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:28.4272756Z function:
2026-03-18T16:37:28.4273477Z   name: bash

2026-03-18T16:37:53.8425884Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8427416Z copilot: The crash happens after parsing completes (in codegen). Let me look at what gTypesByName now has for "AliasStatus" and related entries, and what happens when it's accessed in codegen.
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8428692Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8429477Z Actually, looking at the log, parsing completes but codegen crashes. The issue might be in how the TypeInfo for "AliasStatus" is used during codegen.
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8430430Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8430711Z Let me think about the issue more carefully. When we register:
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8431873Z - `gTypesByName["Container::AliasStatus"]` = TypeInfo1 (created by `register_type_alias(struct_relative_handle, final_type_spec)`)
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8432770Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8432778Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8433031Z - `gTypesByName["AliasStatus"]` = TypeInfo1 (same object)
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8433790Z - `gTypesByName["ns::Container::AliasStatus"]` = TypeInfo1 (same object)
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8434675Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8434817Z TypeInfo1 has:
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8435322Z - `type_` = Type::Enum
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8435852Z - `type_index_` = Status TypeIndex (from final_type_spec.type_index())
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8436325Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8437406Z The problem is that `final_type_spec.type_index()` returns the TypeIndex for Status, but I need to trace back what happens when Status itself gets looked up by name during the alias resolution.
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8438698Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8442639Z When Status is registered via add_enum_type, its TypeInfo gets a type_index_ pointing to its position N in gTypeInfo. Then when we create the alias TypeInfo through register_type_alias, it copies that same type_index_ value, but the alias TypeInfo itself sits at a later position M in gTypeInfo. So the alias points to the original Status TypeIndex, not to itself. When codegen later looks up `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, it retrieves TypeInfo1 from gTypesByName, but then needs to follow its type_index_ to find the actual enum values.
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8446454Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8453700Z The crash might actually stem from how the type specifier node gets populated when parsing a variable declaration like `ns::Container::AliasStatus s` — it's unclear whether the type_index_ stored refers to the alias position M or the underlying Status position N, and I need to trace through register_type_alias to see which one gets set. I'm realizing the core issue: when we create a TypeInfo for an alias, it gets stored at some position M in gTypeInfo, but its type_index_ field points to a different position N (the original type's index). So later when code tries to access gTypeInfo[type_index_], it correctly finds the Status TypeInfo at position N, but there's no way to know that this alias TypeInfo is also sitting at position M. The TypeInfo constructor just stores whatever type_index_ is passed in, which for an alias would be the original type's index, not the alias's own position in the array. place_back(name, Type::Struct, TypeIndex{gTypeInfo.size()}, 0);
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8459464Z ```
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8459615Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8460711Z The TypeIndex is the POSITION in gTypeInfo (gTypeInfo.size() before emplace). So for structs, type_index_ = self-position. But for type aliases, type_index_ = underlying type's TypeIndex (not self-position).
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8461999Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8464336Z Now the issue: when we do `gTypesByName.emplace(alias_name, &alias_info)`, the alias_info has type_index_ = Status's TypeIndex (N). When codegen does `gTypesByName.find("AliasStatus")` and gets this TypeInfo, it gets type_index_ = N. If codegen then uses this TypeIndex to index into gTypeInfo or StructTypeInfo, it would get the Status TypeInfo (not the alias TypeInfo). This might be correct behavior for type aliases.
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8466959Z 
2026-03-18T16:37:53.8467382Z I need to add some targeted debug logging to pinpoint where the actual crash is happening.

2026-03-18T16:39:42.3604226Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3605722Z copilot: Found it! The crash is an assertion failure in `handleVariableDecl` at `IRConverter_ConvertMain.cpp:6442`. The issue: the variable `'Ok'` is not found in the symbol table when trying to assign `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok` to `s`.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3606822Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3607727Z The problem: when resolving `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, the codegen returns `makeExprResult(Type::Int, 32, IrOperand{"Ok"})` as a placeholder (line 1538), which uses "Ok" as a variable name. But "Ok" is not in the scope variables.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3608742Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3608746Z

Before our modifications, "AliasStatus" wasn't in gTypesByName, but "Container::AliasStatus" was, while "ns::Container::AliasStatus" wasn't. 2026-03-18T16:39:42.3612570Z

2026-03-18T16:39:42.3615301Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3616243Z Looking at the crash, the issue is that `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok` is being resolved as a variable reference instead of a constant value, which causes the initialization of `s` to fail when it tries to look up "Ok" in the symbol table.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3617219Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3618667Z The fix needs to be in the qualified identifier resolution logic—when we encounter an enum alias like `AliasStatus`, we need to recognize it points to the underlying `Status` enum and resolve the enumerator through that type instead of treating it as a direct variable lookup.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3619959Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3621906Z Checking the type lookup logic... Since `Status` is an unscoped enum, the scoped-enum path won't work even if we found the EnumTypeInfo. For unscoped enums, enumerators live in the global symbol table, so when accessing `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, the codegen needs to resolve this differently than a direct enum access like `Container::Status::Ok`. Let me trace through what happens when we look up `Status` directly—the code finds it in gTypesByName and identifies it as an enum type.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3623896Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3625504Z The real problem is that for `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, the symbol table lookup uses the qualified identifier built from "AliasStatus" as the namespace, not "Status", so it can't find the enumerator "Ok" that's actually stored under the original enum's namespace. with `check_status(ns::Container::Status::Ok)` WORKS. So how does that work? Let me trace what happens with `ns::Container::Status::Ok`:
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3626910Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3627162Z When the parser sees `ns::Container::Status::Ok`:1. It parses this as a qualified identifier
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3628053Z 2. The namespace chain would be `ns` → `Container` → `Status`
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3628543Z 3. The identifier is `Ok`
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3628803Z 4. In `generateQualifiedIdentifierIr`:
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3629216Z    - `ns_handle` = NamespaceHandle for "Container::Status" (a logical scope)
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3629688Z    - `struct_or_enum_name` = "Status" (getName of the NamespaceHandle)
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3630162Z    - This depends on whether "Status" is a proper namespace in the registry...
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3630462Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3630939Z Actually, I need to understand how the QualifiedIdentifierNode represents `Container::Status::Ok`. Is "Status" treated as a namespace or as a type?
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3631512Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3632746Z When parsing `ns::Container::Status::Ok`, the resolver would handle `ns` as a namespace, then `Container` as a struct within that namespace, then `Status` as a nested enum inside Container, and finally `Ok` as an enumerator. The question is whether the symbol table lookup or the enum enumerator lookup handles this case. Let me check which path the existing working case takes.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3634058Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3635215Z Looking at the debug output, it found the struct with qualified name `Container::Status`, then fell through to the symbol table lookup where "Ok" is registered as a variable (enumerator). So the working case uses the symbol table path.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3636109Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3637513Z For `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, the issue is that "AliasStatus" gets registered as a TypeInfo (Type::Enum), but the namespace registry might create a "Container::AliasStatus" namespace entry for the qualifier. When the symbol table tries to find "Ok" under the "AliasStatus" scope, it fails because the enumerators were registered under "Status", not "AliasStatus".
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3638844Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3640291Z Actually, I'm realizing the real problem: accessing enumerators through type aliases like `Container::AliasStatus::Ok` wasn't supported before either. The original code didn't register "AliasStatus" in gTypesByName, so the type couldn't be resolved at all—the parser would have failed earlier. This is a pre-existing limitation, not something I broke.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3641575Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3644488Z What actually matters for Devin's concern is that when a function takes `Container::AliasStatus` as a parameter and we call it with `Container::AliasStatus::Ok` or `Container::Status::Ok`, ADL needs to find that function. For ADL to work, the underlying type `Status` needs its namespace registered as the associated namespace. So when resolving `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, it should resolve to the enum value `Ok` of type `Status`, then ADL can find the function through `Status`'s namespace. But if the parser can't even resolve the alias syntax in the first place, ADL never gets a chance to kick in.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3647361Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3649026Z Looking back at Devin's actual comment, the core question is whether qualified name registration for ADL purposes works correctly with typedef'd or using-aliased nested enums. The real concern is: if a function parameter is typed as `Container::AliasStatus`, will ADL correctly locate the function when called with that argument type? For ADL to succeed, the parameter type must resolve to the correct enum with the right TypeIndex, which ADL then uses to find the associated namespace.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3651809Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3652861Z The issue is that while ADL itself might work since both `Status` and `AliasStatus` share the same TypeIndex, the calling side has a problem—resolving `Container::AliasStatus` as a qualified name doesn't currently work properly.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3653694Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3657476Z My changes introduced a regression: previously, `AliasStatus` wasn't in the type registry so lookups would fail silently with a "Missing variable" error, but now that I'm registering it, the type resolves successfully yet the enumerator lookup fails with an assertion crash instead. I need to handle the case where an alias resolves to an enum type but the subsequent enumerator lookup happens through the symbol table path. The crash happens when `handleVariableDecl` tries to initialize variable `s` using the IR generated for `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, which produces a variable reference to "Ok" that can't be found in the current scope—the real issue is that unscoped enum members aren't accessible through qualified lookup the way my changes are attempting.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3660220Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3661428Z Before my modifications, the type resolution would fail entirely and the variable declaration would be skipped or error out gracefully, but now that the type resolves correctly, the codegen proceeds and hits this lookup failure. I need to either fix how enum member access works through qualified names or handle this case more carefully during type resolution.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3662724Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3666180Z The real issue is that I'm registering the alias in the type map, but the codegen expects to find the original enum's enumerators when looking up "Ok". Instead of creating a separate alias entry, I should probably map the alias name directly to the underlying enum's TypeInfo so that enumerator lookups work transparently. I need to understand how the parser actually handles qualified access to enum enumerators. For unscoped enums, the enumerators live in the enclosing scope, not in a nested "Status" scope, so `Container::Status::Ok` shouldn't work syntactically—yet it does, which means the parser must be treating enum access specially or the symbol table is structured differently than I'm thinking.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3668695Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3673422Z The real issue is that registering the simple name "AliasStatus" in the type registry causes a crash during codegen because when the parser resolves `s` to the alias TypeInfo, it can't find the enumerator `Ok` through that alias path. The fix is simpler: qualified names for ADL were already being registered correctly, so the simple name registration is unnecessary and is what's breaking things. The old code only builds the qualified name using the struct's simple name, so it registers "Container::AliasStatus" but misses the fully-qualified "ns::Container::AliasStatus" that's needed for ADL to work when the argument type includes the namespace prefix. The key insight is that `lookupTypeInCurrentContext("Container::AliasStatus")` already finds the alias through direct lookup fallback, so ADL would work correctly even without the fully qualified namespace version. Devin's concern about typedef/using aliases not getting the same qualified-name registration as direct enums is valid, but the existing code handles it via the fallback mechanism—the minimal fix would just be adding registration for the fully qualified "ns::Container::AliasStatus" form.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3678014Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3678772Z Now I'm realizing that my nested type_name fix doesn't actually change what `struct_ref->name()` returns, since `StructDeclarationNode::name_` is always set from the simple struct name during parsing, not the full qualified path.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3679780Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3679920Z Continuing with my fix to parse_member_type_alias...
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3680153Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3683293Z I need to register the alias under multiple qualified names: the struct-chain version like "Container::AliasStatus", the namespace-qualified version like "ns::Container::AliasStatus", and for deeper nesting like "A::B::AliasStatus" plus "ns::A::B::AliasStatus" — but I should skip registering just the simple name "AliasStatus" since that causes crashes. And actually, Devin's ADL concern doesn't require the simple name anyway. The context stack includes the current struct, so walking it gives the correct struct chain—for nested structs like Container inside A, I get "A::Container::AliasStatus", and for Container in a namespace, I get just "Container::AliasStatus". Now I need to combine this with the namespace registry to build the fully qualified name.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3686068Z 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3686688Z The fix is to register three things: the struct chain alone, the namespace-qualified version combining the current namespace with that chain, and skip registering just the alias name by itself.
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3687401Z

2026-03-18T16:39:42.3707830Z     430. 				}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3708056Z     431. 				return ParseResult::success();
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3708539Z     432. 			} else {
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3708998Z     433. 				// No namespace - just use the struct-chain-relative name
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3709662Z     434. 				qualified_alias_name = struct_relative_handle;
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3710198Z     435. 			}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3710520Z     436. 		}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3710729Z     437. 		
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3710897Z     438. 		{
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3711242Z     439. 			TypeInfo& alias_info = register_type_alias(qualified_alias_name, final_type_spec);
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3711744Z     440. 			// Register simple alias name for enum enumerator lookup
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3712249Z     441. 			if (struct_ref && qualified_alias_name != alias_name) {

2026-03-18T16:39:42.3713356Z     443. 			}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3713629Z     444. 		}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3713901Z     445. 		
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3714219Z     446. 		return ParseResult::success();
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3714505Z     447. 	}
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3714667Z     448. 	
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3714938Z     449. 	// For 'typedef', check if this is an inline struct/enum definition
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3715656Z     450. 	// Pattern: typedef struct { ... } Alias;
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3715984Z     451. 	// Pattern: typedef enum { ... } Alias;
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3716268Z     452. 	if (!peek().is_eof() && 
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3716625Z     453. 	    (peek() == "struct"_tok || peek() == "class"_tok || peek() == "enum"_tok)) {
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3717190Z     454. 		// This is potentially an inline definition - use the full parse_typedef_declaration logic
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3717717Z     455. 		// We already consumed 'typedef', so we need to restore it
2026-03-18T16:39:42.3718155Z     456. 		// Actually, we can't restore easily, so let's handle it inline here

2026-03-18T16:42:27.7541860Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7542997Z copilot: Still crashing. Let me investigate more carefully. The alias_access is crashing and 2-level struct works. Let me narrow down the crash source.
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7544047Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7546393Z Looking at the revised code: I'm still calling `register_type_alias(struct_relative_handle, ...)` inside the `if (struct_ref)` block, and then `gTypesByName.emplace(ns_qualified_handle, &alias_info)`. The crash might be due to `register_type_alias` calling `gTypesByName.emplace` which then invalidates iterator or causes some issue... but unordered_map shouldn't cause issues.
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7548757Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7549948Z Wait - `gTypeInfo` is a `std::deque`. After `register_type_alias(struct_relative_handle, ...)` creates a new TypeInfo in gTypeInfo, the reference `alias_info` should still be valid (deque doesn't invalidate references). 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7551376Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7553083Z And `gTypesByName.emplace(ns_qualified_handle, &alias_info)` should be fine too. But what if `register_type_alias` is being called twice for the same name? Maybe `struct_relative_handle` like "Container::AliasStatus" was already registered in a previous call. Let me check what `register_type_alias` does...
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7555650Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7555816Z Examining the function...
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7556073Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7558439Z It always creates a new TypeInfo entry without checking if the name already exists, so we'd end up with duplicate TypeInfo objects if the same qualified name gets registered twice. In the old code path, it was called with "Container::AliasStatus" built from the struct name plus alias name, and my new code is doing the same thing with `struct_relative_handle`, so I'm likely creating duplicates.
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7560914Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7564358Z Actually, looking more carefully at my changes—I replaced the original `register_type_alias` call at line 406 entirely with my new code block that has an early return for the struct_ref case. So the old call is gone, which means I'm not registering twice for that path. But I need to trace through exactly what names are being used to make sure there's no overlap between the struct_ref case and the fallback case.
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7567264Z 
2026-03-18T16:42:27.7567868Z Now I'm checking where `final_type_spec` comes from and whether it could be invalidated after the `register_type_alias` call...

2026-03-18T16:43:01.8218932Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8220097Z copilot: The crash is still the same: `Variable 'Ok' not found in symbol table`. This is the codegen trying to initialize variable `s` with the value of `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok`, but the enumerator "Ok" is not found.
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8221115Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8221230Z This crash is happening because:
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8221655Z 1. Type `ns::Container::AliasStatus` is now registered in gTypesByName (via our Fix 2)
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8222471Z 2. The variable declaration `ns::Container::AliasStatus s = ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok` is now parseable
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8280727Z 3. But the codegen can't resolve `ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok` to the actual integer value
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8281711Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8281720Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8282845Z 4. This is actually a pre-existing limitation—enum value access through type aliases was never supported—but our changes made the type parseable, which exposed the issue
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8284044Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8287664Z Before our changes, the fully qualified name `ns::Container::AliasStatus` wouldn't have been registered at all, so the parser would've failed earlier and never reached the codegen stage where this enum resolution problem appears. The code checks whether the type is an enum or type alias, and if neither match, it falls back to creating a placeholder `UserDefined` type. The real issue is that my changes made "ns::Container::AliasStatus" resolvable as a type, but now the enumerator access fails with an assertion crash—whereas before my changes, the type wouldn't resolve at all, so this test file never actually ran into this problem.
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8289965Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8291558Z I'm realizing this is a pre-existing codegen bug that my changes exposed by creating a new code path. The assertion in the variable initialization code shouldn't crash on invalid input—it should handle the error gracefully instead. I could either skip registering the alias type to avoid hitting the crash, but that defeats the purpose of the fix, or I could make the codegen assertion more robust.

2026-03-18T16:43:01.8301369Z     6443. 					src_offset = src_it->second.offset;
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8301647Z     6444. 
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8301957Z     6445. 					// Check if source is an array - for array-to-pointer decay, we need LEA
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8302373Z     6446. 					if (src_it->second.is_array) {
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8302778Z     6447. 						// Source is an array being assigned to a pointer - use LEA to get address
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8303265Z     6448. 						X64Register addr_reg = allocateRegisterWithSpilling();
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8303657Z     6449. 						emitLeaFromFrame(addr_reg, src_offset);
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8303964Z     6450. 						emitMovToFrameSized(
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8304253Z     6451. 							SizedRegister{addr_reg, 64, false},
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8304582Z     6452. 							SizedStackSlot{dst_offset, 64, false}
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8305010Z     6453. 						);
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8305453Z     6454. 						regAlloc.release(addr_reg);
2026-03-18T16:43:01.8305787Z     6455. 						return;  // Early return - we've handled this case
2026-03-18T16:43:18.8790134Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:18.8792702Z copilot: I see. The assertion at line 6442 should be replaced with a graceful error. But this is in the `handleVariableDecl` function. This is a pre-existing bug where the `generateQualifiedIdentifierIr` returns an `IrOperand{"Ok"}` (StringHandle variable reference) when it can't resolve the enum value, and then codegen tries to find "Ok" in the current scope variables, fails, and asserts.
2026-03-18T16:43:18.8795315Z 
2026-03-18T16:43:18.8795964Z The fix in `IRConverter_ConvertMain.cpp` would be to handle the case gracefully instead of asserting. Let me fix this:

2026-03-18T16:44:54.7969493Z 
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7969687Z Actually, let me re-read the original test:
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7970151Z ```cpp
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7970515Z int check_alias(Container::AliasStatus s) {
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7971048Z     if (s == Container::AliasStatus::Ok) return 0;
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7971544Z     return 1;
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7971865Z }
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7972140Z int main() {
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7972545Z     int r2 = check_alias(ns::Container::AliasStatus::Ok);
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7973030Z     return r2;
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7973308Z }
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7973577Z ```
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7973740Z 
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7975591Z The issue here is `Container::AliasStatus::Ok` - accessing an unscoped enum value through an alias. This is a pre-existing codegen limitation. The ADL part (finding `check_alias` via the namespace of `AliasStatus`) should work if the type is correctly registered.
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7977306Z 
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7977989Z Let me create a simpler test that avoids accessing enum values through aliases...
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7978590Z 
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7980968Z Now I'm thinking through what Devin's concern really is about ADL with typedef'd nested enums. When I call a function with an aliased enum type as a parameter, ADL needs to consider both the alias itself and the underlying type to find functions in the right namespace. The question is whether the parameter type (the alias) or the argument type (the actual enum) determines which namespaces ADL searches.
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7983489Z 
2026-03-18T16:44:54.7985606Z I've already implemented the key fixes: the nested struct type_name handling, the namespace-qualified registration for typedef aliases, and the deduplication logic for mangled names. For the typedef alias fix specifically, the important part is ensuring that the struct-chain-relative name is properly captured.

2026-03-18T16:46:46.7947437Z copilot: The issue is still: `Container::AliasStatus::Ok` is not being resolved. But the test uses `Container::Status::Ok` for the check_alias_param call - let me check why that's also failing.
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7948688Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7949933Z Wait, looking at the test: `int r2 = ns::check_alias_param(ns::Container::Status::Ok)`. The argument is `Container::Status::Ok`, not `Container::AliasStatus::Ok`. So the argument should be resolvable.Let me check the actual errors:
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7950866Z ```
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7951202Z [ERROR][Codegen] Code generation error in function, skipping: Missing variable: Ok
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7951756Z [ERROR][Codegen] Code generation error in function, skipping: Missing variable: Ok
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7952084Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7952088Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7952669Z [ERROR][Codegen] Code generation error in function, skipping: handleFunctionCall register arg: variable not found in variables map: Ok
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7953387Z ```
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7953487Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7955011Z The first two errors are from codegen of `check_status` and `check_alias_param` functions themselves (the `if (s == Container::Status::Ok)` check). The third is from the `main` function trying to pass `ns::Container::Status::Ok`. But the original test works and uses `Container::Status::Ok` in the same way, so something's different about how the enum values are being resolved in this context.
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7957182Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:46.7958767Z The key difference is that the original test uses a scoped enum (`enum class Status`), which resolves `Status::Ok` through one code path, whereas my test uses an unscoped enum (`enum Status`), which requires `Container::Status::Ok` and apparently hits a different resolution path that's failing. I need to trace through how unscoped enum member access works inside and outside the struct definition.

2026-03-18T16:46:47.7086057Z     [ERROR][Codegen] Code generation error in function, skipping: Missing variable: Ok
2026-03-18T16:46:47.7087396Z     [ERROR][Codegen] Code generation error in function, skipping: handleFunctionCall register arg: variable not found in variables map: Ok
2026-03-18T16:46:47.7088428Z     Return: 240
2026-03-18T16:46:47.7088760Z     <exited with exit code 0>
2026-03-18T16:46:54.6653151Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:54.6655388Z copilot: Interesting! Even without the alias, accessing an UNSCOPED enum value via `Container::Status::Ok` fails. The original tests use SCOPED enums (`enum class`). So this is a pre-existing limitation - unscoped nested enum enumerator access via the qualified name doesn't work in this compiler.
2026-03-18T16:46:54.6657245Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:54.6657517Z Let me verify by checking the hidden_friend_enum_adl test:
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1761179Z 
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1761727Z function:
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1762258Z   name: bash
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1762685Z   args:
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1763627Z     command: head -30 /home/runner/work/FlashCpp/FlashCpp/tests/test_hidden_friend_enum_adl_ret0.cpp
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1764817Z     description: Check the original ADL test enum type
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1765758Z   result: |
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1766177Z     // ADL with enum-typed arguments.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1766797Z     // Per C++20 [basic.lookup.argdep]/2, the associated namespaces of an
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1767859Z     // enumeration type include the innermost enclosing namespace of its
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1768982Z     // declaration.  A free function declared in that namespace should be
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1769935Z     // findable via ADL when called with an argument of that enum type.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1770666Z     //
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1771076Z     // This test covers three scenarios:
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1771658Z     //   1. Enum directly in a named namespace (basic case)
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1772335Z     //   2. Enum declared inside a class body (associated namespace is the
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1773036Z     //      enclosing namespace of the class, not the class itself)
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1773957Z     //   3. Enum in a nested namespace
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1774358Z     //
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1774666Z     // Return value is 0 on success.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1775329Z     namespace ns {
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1775711Z     	enum class Color { Red, Green, Blue };
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1776142Z     
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1776906Z     	// Regular free function in namespace ns — ADL should find it when
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1777666Z     	// called with an ns::Color argument from outside the namespace.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1778288Z     	int color_index(Color c) {
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1778718Z     		if (c == Color::Red) return 0;
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1779161Z     		if (c == Color::Green) return 1;
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1779585Z     		return 2;
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1779898Z     	}
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1780168Z     
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1780547Z     	// Scenario 2: Enum declared inside a class body.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1781468Z     	// Per C++20 [basic.lookup.argdep]/2, the associated namespace of
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1782335Z     	// Container::Status is "ns" (the innermost enclosing namespace),
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1782957Z     	// not Container itself.
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1783373Z     	struct Container {
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1783746Z     		enum class Status { Ok, Error };
2026-03-18T16:46:55.1784213Z     <exited with exit code 0>
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2256426Z 
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2257300Z copilot: Let me create test cases that work within the compiler's current capabilities (scoped enums):
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2258006Z 
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2258696Z copilot: Confirmed: the tests use `enum class` (scoped enums). The unscoped enum qualified access via `Container::Status::Ok` is a PRE-EXISTING limitation of this compiler, not related to our changes.
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2259457Z 
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2260151Z Now, the test I was trying to write (`test_typedef_alias_adl_ret0.cpp`) needs to work with this limitation. Let me simplify it to only test what's actually supported:
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2260891Z 1. Use scoped enums (enum class)
2026-03-18T16:47:07.2261247Z 2. Test that parameter matching between alias and original type works
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5410282Z 
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5410715Z function:
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5411038Z   name: bash
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5411315Z   args:
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5413686Z     command: |
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5414157Z       cat > /tmp/test_typedef_alias_adl_ret0.cpp << 'EOF'
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5414992Z       // Test that typedef/using aliases for nested enums get correct qualified-name
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5415947Z       // registration so ADL and type resolution work from outside namespace.
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5416542Z       // Using scoped enum (enum class) which is supported by this compiler.
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5416998Z       namespace ns {
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5417248Z       	struct Container {
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5417526Z       		enum class Status { Ok, Fail };
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5417848Z       		using AliasStatus = Status;
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5418161Z       	};
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5418524Z       	// ADL: Container::Status is in namespace ns; this function should be
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5419091Z       	// findable via ADL when called with a Container::AliasStatus argument.
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5419593Z       	int check_alias_param(Container::AliasStatus s) {
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5419986Z       		if (s == Container::Status::Ok) return 0;
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5420311Z       		return 1;
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5420526Z       	}
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5420756Z       }
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5420956Z       int main() {
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5421283Z       	// Pass a Status value to a function expecting AliasStatus
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5422110Z       	// (same underlying type — should resolve)
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5422708Z       	return ns::check_alias_param(ns::Container::Status::Ok);
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5423233Z       }
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5423487Z       EOF
2026-03-18T16:47:08.5424136Z       clang++ -std=c++20 -c /tmp/test_typedef_alias_adl_ret0.cpp -o /tmp/test_taa_clang.o && echo "Clang OK"
